turns.content: I'm trying to build a Rust tool that automates repetitive tasks. Should I just use std::panic::set_hook for capturing backtraces?

Good question! Using `std::panic::set_hook` is one way to capture backtraces, but it's worth considering whether that fits your needs fully. Sometimes, relying solely on panics can be overkill for more graceful error handling.
But I want the tool to fail gracefully if something goes wrong. So is `std::panic::set_hook` enough?

Actually, just using panic hooks won't give you a user-friendly error message by default; it will still terminate your program abruptly. For better UX and debugging, I recommend combining panic hooks with the `human-panic` crate to provide more readable errors.
Oh right, so I should use both then?

Yes! First, set up a panic hook with `std::panic::set_hook` to capture backtraces. Then integrate the `human-panic` crate for nicer output. This way, you get detailed debugging information plus more friendly error messages that can be customized.
Hey, I'm learning Rust and I want to handle errors gracefully. Can you tell me about panic hooks?

Sure! In Rust, panics are essentially runtime errors that cause the program to stop abruptly unless explicitly handled. Sometimes you might want your program to do something more user-friendly when a panic occurs rather than just crashing.
So I can set up some kind of handler? Like capturing backtraces?

Absolutely! You can use `std::panic::set_hook` to register a custom panic hook. This lets you capture detailed information, like the call stack at the time of the panic, which is very useful for debugging.
Okay, so I just wrap my code in try-catch blocks then?

No, that's not quite right. Rust doesn't use traditional `try-catch`. Instead, you'd typically return errors explicitly from functions and propagate them up the call stack using `Result` or `Option`. But if you really want a panic hook for unexpected situations, yes, you can set one with `std::panic::set_hook`. It's more like an emergency override.
Hmm, I see. And what about capturing backtraces? Is that built-in?
Yes! The `backtrace` crate provides utilities for getting the call stack information when a panic occurs. You can integrate this with your custom hook to print out detailed traces.
Exactly! And inside that closure, you can use `backtrace::Backtrace::capture()` to get the current stack trace. Then format it however you like and print it out or save it.
